text: Hi, this is Abu Bakr Ali. I'm showing a video here with some interesting findings. This is a patient who was admitted to the hospital with obstructive jaundice. He was found to have a very large pseudocyst that was actually displacing D-II, as you can see on the CAT scan, causing external obstruction of the common bile duct. His total bilirubin was initially twelve. He had this large pseudocyst. He had stones in his gallbladder. A lot of people argue, you know, like it's better to kind of send these patients to GI to do, to place a stent or surgery to kind of place a stent, an axia stent. I like doing these surgically. Usually if everything goes fine, these patients are discharged in twenty-four hours. You can take out the gallbladder. You can do a necrosectomy. It's a one time, it's a one thing, it's a one procedure.

The surgeon performs an intraoperative ultrasound to locate a massive cyst, which ascends above the lesser curve and displaces the second part of the duodenum laterally. Despite the displacement, the patient shows no symptoms of gastric outlet obstruction. The surgeon then performs an anterior gastrotomy in the pre-pyloric area, suturing the superior flap for better exposure. The posterior wall is opened, and some bleeding is encountered.

The surgeon attempts to control bleeding at the surgical site by using bipolar cautery, but the bleeding persists. The surgeon applies suction and pressure while preparing to place a figure-eight stitch to stop the bleeding.

The surgeon applies pressure to control bleeding and places a figure-eight suture using a 3-0 Prolene stitch to slow down the bleeding. The cyst is opened to allow for a full-thickness bite, providing better visualization of the surgical area.
The surgeon carefully opens the cyst wall using electrocautery, ensuring there is no bleeding during the process. The cyst is decompressed as the wall is progressively opened further.

The surgeon uses a V-lock stitch to suture the thick wall of the pseudocyst to the posterior wall of the stomach, ensuring the cyst remains open for drainage. Care is taken to avoid suturing the anterior stomach wall to prevent gastric outlet obstruction. The surgeon places additional stitches to control bleeding and enlarges the opening, sending a burnt portion of the wall for pathology confirmation.

The surgeon completes suturing and performs a necrosectomy, removing necrotic tissue from the pseudocyst. The tissue is sent to pathology for analysis. The anterior wall is closed, and an EGD is performed to ensure there is no obstruction in the pre-pyloric area, visually confirming the cyst gastrostomy.

The surgeon inserts a scope, revealing a significant amount of bile discharge. Following a cholecystectomy, a wire is passed under fluoroscopic guidance to ensure it enters the duodenum, with occasional reflux into the stomach.

Under fluoroscopy, the surgeon passes a temporary French seven-centimeter double pigtail catheter, ensuring the stent is loaded backwards with a distal radio marker and another marker on the blue deployment device. The surgeon adjusts the catheter's position by pulling it back to align the proximal radio marker correctly before firing and clipping the cystic duct. The stent is visible post-operatively.
